title: Introduction to R for Data Analysis: Forensic Edition
description: Master R programming through forensic science applications. Explore DNA profiling, ballistics analysis, and crime scene statistics with hands-on forensic case studies.
topic: Introduction to R for Data Analysis

nodes.title: R in Forensic Science: A Data-Driven Approach to Evidence Analysis
nodes.content: Forensic science increasingly relies on data analysis to extract meaningful patterns from evidence. R is a powerful statistical programming language that forensic laboratories use to analyze DNA profiles, ballistics patterns, chemical traces, and crime scene distributions. In this course, you'll learn R fundamentals through three critical forensic applications: genetic evidence analysis, projectile trajectory analysis, and statistical crime scene modeling. By mastering data import, manipulation, and visualization in R, you'll develop skills that forensic professionals use daily to strengthen their testimony and solve cases.

nodes.title: Choose Your Forensic Specialty
nodes.content: Which area of forensic analysis interests you most? Each path teaches essential R skills through real-world forensic scenarios.

nodes.title: DNA Profiling: R for Genetic Evidence Analysis
nodes.content: DNA analysis produces large datasets of allelic profiles, genetic markers, and population frequency data. In forensic casework, analysts compare suspect DNA profiles against crime scene evidence using statistical calculations like match probabilities and likelihood ratios. R excels at importing genotype files from CODIS (Combined DNA Index System), computing allele frequencies from reference populations, and calculating the probability that a DNA profile came from a random, unrelated individual. You'll learn how to load DNA data into R, filter for the 13 core loci, and compute statistical measures that courts rely on to evaluate DNA evidence strength in criminal trials.

nodes.title: Worked Example: Calculating DNA Match Probability
nodes.content: A forensic lab receives DNA evidence from a crime scene and a suspect. The evidence shows matching profiles at all 13 CODIS loci. The analyst uses R to import allele frequency data from the FBI's population database, calculate observed frequencies for each allele using dplyr, and compute the combined probability. Using the product rule (multiplying individual locus probabilities under Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium), they calculate: 1 in 50 billion. This calculation demonstrates how R transforms raw genetic data into a statistic that expert witnesses explain to juries, connecting data manipulation skills to real courtroom testimony.

nodes.title: Ballistics Analysis: R for Projectile & Trace Pattern Recognition
nodes.content: Ballistic evidence—bullet striations, cartridge case markings, and distance patterns—generates spatial and morphological data that forensic examiners compare across cases. R can import 3D surface profiles from automated comparison microscopes, compute spatial correlations between bullet striations, analyze shot-pattern dispersions, and identify potential firearm matches across databases of thousands of recovered projectiles. Modern ballistics examination uses R to extract quantitative surface topology features, normalize them for comparison, and rank suspect firearms by match strength. This data-driven approach helps investigators prioritize leads and prosecutors strengthen firearms evidence, moving beyond subjective microscopy comparison to rigorous statistical evaluation.

nodes.title: Worked Example: Matching Bullet Striations Across Crime Scenes
nodes.content: Two armed robberies occur two months apart. The ballistics examiner uses R to import 3D confocal microscopy scans of the bullet lands and grooves from both crime scenes—each scan containing 50,000+ topographic data points. Using R's correlation-based analysis, they overlay the profiles, compute cross-correlation scores at different rotation angles, and identify a peak correlation of 0.94. R's ggplot2 generates overlay visualization showing perfectly aligned striations. The analyst queries a regional firearms database of 12,000 previously recovered bullets using R, and the suspect firearm from robbery one ranks as the top match for robbery two bullets (correlation 0.91). This evidence links the cases and prioritizes suspect investigation.

nodes.title: Crime Scene Statistics: R for Spatial Analysis & Geographic Patterns
nodes.content: Crime scene analysts document the precise location of evidence items—blood spatters, fibers, trace evidence, tool marks—in 2D and 3D coordinate systems. R can import spatial data from total station measurements or 3D photogrammetry, calculate distance metrics between evidence clusters, identify hotspots of activity using kernel density estimation, and model spatial relationships between evidence and critical locations like entry/exit points or areas of struggle. When crimes occur in series, R helps analysts compute the geographic distribution of incidents to reveal patterns, predict likely suspect residence or workplace using spatial statistics, and generate heat maps that guide investigative focus. This transforms crime scenes from descriptive photographs into quantitative spatial datasets.

nodes.title: Worked Example: Serial Burglary Geographic Profiling
nodes.content: A police department experiences 23 residential burglaries over six months. The analyst uses R to import GPS coordinates of each burgled home, calculates pairwise distance matrices, and applies kernel density estimation to identify geographic clustering. R's ggplot2 mapping functions visualize the burglary hotspots, revealing a dense concentration in the northeast quadrant. The analyst overlays street networks, calculates distance to public transit, and models distance decay functions. Based on this spatial analysis using R's sp and sf packages, investigators hypothesize the burglar lives or works within the 2-mile radius where crime probability peaks. This geographic profile generates actionable investigative leads and dramatically narrows search area compared to random patrol.

nodes.title: Integrating Multiple Data Sources: The Complete Forensic Analysis Workflow
nodes.content: Real forensic investigations combine multiple evidence types. A suspect may be linked to a crime scene through DNA match probability, ballistics correlation, and spatial proximity—each providing independent evidence of association. R's strength lies in integrating these diverse data streams: genetic profiles, ballistic topographies, and crime scene coordinates can all be processed, visualized, and cross-referenced in a single analytical workflow. You've learned how R handles DNA frequencies, ballistic striations, and geographic distributions. Now you'll synthesize these skills to analyze a realistic scenario where an analyst must coordinate evidence from multiple forensic disciplines, produce integrated results, and create a coherent narrative for prosecution.

nodes.title: Integrated Forensic Case Analysis: Demonstrating Mastery
nodes.content: You are a forensic analyst evaluating a complex burglary case with three evidence sources. DNA evidence from a broken window yields a match probability of 1 in 10 million. Bullets recovered from the residence match the suspect's registered firearm with a correlation of 0.92. The burglary location falls within 1.5 miles of the suspect's address, placing it in the top 5% of the geographic crime hotspot from kernel density mapping. Your task is to interpret each evidence type using your R knowledge and synthesize the findings into a coherent case narrative.

questions.question: A DNA match probability of 1 in 10 million means:
options: The suspect definitely committed the crime
There's a 1 in 10 million chance the DNA came from an unrelated person in the population
The lab made an error in analysis
The DNA evidence cannot be used in court
feedback: Incorrect—match probability statistics measure likelihood, not certainty
Correct—this is the technical definition of match probability used in forensic genetics
Incorrect—quality DNA analysis produces reliable statistical measures
Incorrect—this statistic is admissible and frequently used in judicial proceedings
questions.explanation: Match probability quantifies how rare a DNA profile is in the population, helping courts understand evidence strength without claiming absolute certainty.

questions.question: If ballistic correlation between the suspect's firearm and evidence bullets is 0.92:
options: The correlation is too low to be meaningful in court
The comparison is inconclusive and requires more data
The striations are nearly identical, supporting a firearm match
Only DNA evidence matters; ballistic evidence is supplementary
feedback: Incorrect—0.92 is an exceptionally high correlation on a 0–1 scale
Incorrect—0.92 indicates high confidence in a ballistic match
Correct—this correlation suggests the suspect's firearm fired the evidence bullets
Incorrect—ballistics provides independent evidence that strengthens the overall case
questions.explanation: A correlation of 0.92 means the striations on evidence bullets align with the suspect's gun's characteristics with 92% similarity—very strong evidence of a match.

questions.question: The suspect's residence is in the top 5% of the geographic crime hotspot. Combined with DNA and ballistics, this suggests:
options: Spatial analysis alone is sufficient to prove guilt
Each independent evidence source strengthens the overall case
The spatial evidence contradicts the DNA and ballistic findings
Geographic profiling is only useful in serial crime series
feedback: Incorrect—no single evidence type proves guilt independently
Correct—multiple independent forensic sources converge to build a substantially stronger case
Incorrect—the spatial location supports and reinforces the DNA and ballistic findings
Incorrect—geographic analysis applies to both single incidents and series crimes
questions.explanation: The convergence of DNA (1 in 10 million), ballistics (0.92 correlation), and geographic proximity (top 5% hotspot) creates powerful cumulative evidence that would be compelling to a jury.

nodes.title: Case Closed: R in Forensic Science Mastery
nodes.content: Congratulations! You've mastered the foundational R skills that forensic scientists use daily to analyze evidence. You understand how to manipulate genetic profiles, compare ballistic patterns, and model crime scene spatial distributions. These skills form the backbone of modern forensic analysis. As you advance, you'll explore more sophisticated statistical techniques, larger forensic databases, and integrated case management systems—all built on the R fundamentals you've learned. Your ability to extract data-driven insights from evidence will strengthen investigations, support prosecution, and advance justice. Now go solve crimes with data!
